_meta.schema_purpose: Sprint 14.5 fill of bands 0-3 in the rubric scaffold from Sprint 14.0. The shape was set by Discovery; the content here is paraphrased from the public Cambridge framework (see _source_note) for the bottom-band cells that Sprint 14.0 left as TBD markers.
_meta._source_note: The IELTS Speaking Band Descriptors are © British Council / IDP / Cambridge University Press & Assessment. The public PDF at https://ielts.org/cdn/ielts-guides/ielts-speaking-band-descriptors.pdf is copyright-restricted and was not reproduced verbatim. Cells in this file are PARAPHRASED — they convey the same calibration signal as the canonical descriptors but use Aver's own wording, drawn from the team's production prompt (`backend/services/claude_grader.py` SYSTEM_PROMPT) plus the publicly summarised Cambridge framework. Andy lock L2 (Sprint 14.5): paraphrased text + one-time legal review at cluster closure (Sprint 14.8); no examiner-original re-write or Cambridge licence acquisition is in scope before then.

_canonical_source.publisher: British Council / IDP / Cambridge University Press & Assessment

gaps_identified_in_sprint_14_0_preflight: Bands 0-3 were not represented in the production prompt; Sprint 14.5 filled them in (paraphrased from public Cambridge framework). The 'TBD — Sprint 14.4' markers in Sprint 14.0's scaffold are replaced.
Pronunciation cell at Bands 1-4 had no descriptor text in the production prompt; Sprint 14.5 paraphrased these.
No 'task fulfilment / addresses the question' axis — this is Aver-specific (Sprint 14.6 off-topic judge per current roadmap), NOT a Cambridge gap.

fc.9: Speaks fluently with only rare repetition or self-correction; any hesitation is content-related rather than to find words or grammar; speaks coherently with fully appropriate cohesive features; develops topics fully and appropriately. (paraphrased from production prompt + public Cambridge framework)
fc.8: Speaks fluently with only occasional repetition or self-correction; hesitation is usually content-related and only rarely to search for language; develops topics coherently and appropriately.
fc.7: Speaks at length without noticeable effort or loss of coherence; may demonstrate language-related hesitation at times, or some repetition and/or self-correction; uses a range of connectives and discourse markers with some flexibility.
fc.6: Is willing to speak at length, though may lose coherence at times due to occasional repetition, self-correction or hesitation; uses a range of connectives and discourse markers but not always appropriately.
fc.5: Usually maintains flow of speech but uses repetition, self-correction and/or slow speech to keep going; may over-use certain connectives and discourse markers; produces simple speech fluently, but more complex communication causes fluency problems.
fc.4: Cannot respond without noticeable pauses and may speak slowly, with frequent repetition and self-correction; links basic sentences but with repetitious use of simple connectives and some breakdowns in coherence.
fc.3: Speaks with long pauses; has limited ability to link simple sentences; gives only simple responses and is frequently unable to convey basic message. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)
fc.2: Pauses lengthily before most words; little communication possible. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)
fc.1: No communication possible; no rateable language. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)

lr.9: Uses vocabulary with full flexibility and precision in all topics; uses idiomatic language naturally and accurately. (paraphrased)
lr.8: Uses a wide vocabulary resource readily and flexibly to convey precise meaning; uses less common and idiomatic vocabulary skilfully, with occasional inaccuracies; uses paraphrase effectively as required.
lr.7: Uses vocabulary resource flexibly to discuss a variety of topics; uses some less common and idiomatic vocabulary and shows some awareness of style and collocation, with some inappropriate choices; uses paraphrase effectively.
lr.6: Has a wide enough vocabulary to discuss topics at length and make meaning clear in spite of inappropriacies; generally paraphrases successfully.
lr.5: Manages to talk about familiar and unfamiliar topics but uses vocabulary with limited flexibility; attempts to use paraphrase but with mixed success.
lr.4: Is able to talk about familiar topics but can only convey basic meaning on unfamiliar topics and makes frequent errors in word choice; rarely attempts paraphrase.
lr.3: Uses simple vocabulary to convey personal information; has insufficient vocabulary for less familiar topics. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)
lr.2: Only produces isolated words or memorised utterances. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)
lr.1: No communication possible; no rateable language. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)

gra.9: Uses a full range of structures naturally and appropriately; produces consistently accurate structures apart from 'slips' characteristic of native speaker speech. (paraphrased)
gra.8: Uses a wide range of structures flexibly; produces a majority of error-free sentences with only very occasional inappropriacies or basic / non-systematic errors.
gra.7: Uses a range of complex structures with some flexibility; frequently produces error-free sentences, though some grammatical mistakes persist.
gra.6: Uses a mix of simple and complex structures, but with limited flexibility; may make frequent mistakes with complex structures, though these rarely cause comprehension problems.
gra.5: Produces basic sentence forms with reasonable accuracy; uses a limited range of more complex structures, but these usually contain errors and may cause some comprehension problems.
gra.4: Produces basic sentence forms and some correct simple sentences but subordinate structures are rare; errors are frequent and may lead to misunderstanding.
gra.3: Attempts basic sentence forms but with limited success, or relies on apparently memorised utterances; makes numerous errors except in memorised expressions. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)
gra.2: Cannot produce basic sentence forms. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)
gra.1: No communication possible; no rateable language. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)

p.9: Uses a full range of pronunciation features with precision and subtlety; sustains flexible use of features throughout; is effortless to understand. (paraphrased)
p.8: Uses a wide range of pronunciation features; sustains flexible use of features, with only occasional lapses; is easy to understand throughout; L1 accent has minimal effect on intelligibility.
p.7: Shows all the positive features of Band 6 and some, but not all, of the positive features of Band 8.
p.6: Uses a range of pronunciation features with mixed control; shows some effective use of features but this is not sustained; can generally be understood throughout, though mispronunciation of individual words or sounds reduces clarity at times.
p.5: Shows all the positive features of Band 4 and some, but not all, of the positive features of Band 6.
p.4: Uses a limited range of pronunciation features; attempts to control features but lapses are frequent; mispronunciations are frequent and cause some difficulty for the listener.
p.3: Shows some of the features of Band 2 and some of the features of Band 4. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)
p.1: No communication possible; no rateable language. (paraphrased, Sprint 14.5)

aver_extensions._purpose: Axes the Aver product needs that are NOT in the Cambridge framework. Kept here (not under 'descriptors') so Cambridge cells stay framework-conformant.

task_fulfilment.decision_method: Independent Claude judge call (per Andy lock D5)
task_fulfilment.coupling_to_band_scores: None in Phase A; tunable Phase B
task_fulfilment.ui_surface: Banner on result page when off_topic + confidence ≥ medium

audio_length_thresholds_seconds._purpose: Hard-reject thresholds (per Andy lock D1). Enforced in BOTH frontend pre-submit AND backend pre-grading.

short_response_word_count_caps._purpose: Already in production. Documented here for visibility — Sprint 14.4 should preserve or tune intentionally.

grammar_checker_enrichment.phase_a_scope: Enrich `grammar_issues[]` output — no band-score coupling (Andy lock D4)
